## plugin:context7:context7
Use this server to fetch current documentation whenever the user asks about a library, framework, SDK, API, CLI tool, or cloud service -- even well-known ones like React, Next.js, Prisma, Express, Tailwind, Django, or Spring Boot. This includes API syntax, configuration, version migration, library-specific debugging, setup instructions, and CLI tool usage. Use even when you think you know the answer -- your training data may not reflect recent changes. Prefer this over web search for library docs.

Do not use for: refactoring, writing scripts from scratch, debugging business logic, code review, or general programming concepts.

content.text:
Good question. That copy ties Sequant exclusively to Claude Code. A few things to consider:

**If Sequant only works with Claude Code today and for the foreseeable future** — the language is accurate and specific, which is good for discoverability (people searching for Claude Code tooling find you). No change needed.

**If you want to leave room for other agents** — you could generalize slightly:

> Sequant is workflow automation for AI coding agents. It turns GitHub issues into merge-ready pull requests through structured phases and quality gates.

This keeps the same structure but widens the aperture without being vague.

**A middle ground** — lead with what it does, mention Claude Code as the current integration:

> Sequant turns GitHub issues into merge-ready pull requests through structured phases and quality gates. Currently built for Claude Code.

This avoids "pigeon-holing" while being honest about what's supported today.

The real question is strategic: do you want to support other agents (Cursor, Copilot Workspace, Aider, etc.) eventually? If yes, generalize now so early adopters don't mentally file it as "Claude Code only." If no, the specificity is a feature, not a bug.

title:	v2.0.0 - MCP Server, /assess Unification & Parallel Execution
tag:	v2.0.0
draft:	false
prerelease:	false
author:	admarble
created:	2026-04-05T22:43:47Z
published:	2026-04-05T22:43:57Z
url:	https://github.com/sequant-io/sequant/releases/tag/v2.0.0
--
## What's Changed

### Breaking Changes
- **`/solve` merged into `/assess`** — `/solve` still works as deprecated alias, removed in v3.0 (#325)
- **GitHub Actions label** `sequant:solve` → `sequant:assess` (both accepted during transition) (#438)
- **TypeScript API:** `findSolveComment` → `findAssessComment` (old name exported as deprecated alias) (#325)

See [migration guide](https://github.com/sequant-io/sequant/blob/main/CHANGELOG.md#migration-from-v1x) for details.

### Features
- **MCP server** — `sequant serve` exposes workflow as MCP tools (`sequant_run`, `sequant_status`, `sequant_logs`) (#372)
- **Parallel execution** — multi-issue runs concurrent by default with `--concurrency` (#404)
- **Aider agent backend** — `--agent aider` as alternative to Claude Code (#369)
- **GitHub Actions integration** — label-triggered and comment-triggered CI workflows (#370)
- **Package manager detection in CLI** — messages adapt to npm/pnpm/yarn/bun (#487)
- **Plugin distribution** — first-class Claude Code plugin with marketplace listing (#476)
- **QA optimization** — diff-based re-runs skip previously MET items (#377)
- **Small-diff QA fast path** — inline checks instead of 3 sub-agents for small diffs (#465)
- **Structured error capture** — `sequant logs --failed` shows categorized failures (#447)
- **Status reconciliation** — `sequant status` syncs with GitHub on every read (#423)
- **Detailed stats** — `sequant stats --detailed` with QA trends and failure forensics (#437)

### Bug Fixes
- Fix `reconcileState()` race condition during parallel runs (#458)
- Fix label matching using exact equality instead of substring (#461)
- Fix AC parser for bold-wrapped ID format (#422)
- Fix concurrent state writes via file locking (#409)
- Fix MCP config generation for multiple clients (#395)

**Full Changelog**: https://github.com/sequant-io/sequant/compare/v1.20.3...v2.0.0

# Sequant

**Workflow automation for AI coding agents.**

Solve GitHub issues with structured phases and quality gates — from issue to merge-ready PR.

**[sequant.io](https://sequant.io)** — docs, guides, and getting started.

[![npm version](https://img.shields.io/npm/v/sequant.svg)](https://www.npmjs.com/package/sequant)
[![License: MIT](https://img.shields.io/badge/License-MIT-green.svg)](https://opensource.org/licenses/MIT)

### What's new in 2.0

- **MCP server** — `sequant serve` exposes workflow orchestration as MCP tools (`sequant_run`, `sequant_status`, `sequant_logs`). Any MCP client can drive Sequant headlessly.
- **`/assess` unification** — `/solve` is merged into `/assess` with a 6-action vocabulary (PROCEED, CLOSE, MERGE, REWRITE, CLARIFY, PARK). `/solve` still works as an alias.
- **Parallel execution** — multi-issue runs are concurrent by default with `--concurrency`.
- **Multi-agent** — `--agent aider` as an alternative backend, with a driver interface for future agents.
- **GitHub Actions** — label-triggered and comment-triggered CI workflows out of the box.

Upgrading from v1.x? See the [migration guide](CHANGELOG.md#migration-from-v1x).

## Quick Start

### Option A: Plugin (interactive users)

In Claude Code:
```
/plugin install sequant@sequant-io/sequant
/sequant:setup
```

You get skills, hooks, and MCP tools — no npm required.

### Option B: Package install (power users / CI)

```bash
npm install sequant          # npm
pnpm add sequant             # pnpm
yarn add sequant             # yarn
bun add sequant              # bun
```

Then initialize:
```bash
npx sequant init     # Install skills to your project
npx sequant doctor   # Verify setup
```

### Start Using

Then in Claude Code:

```
/fullsolve 123    # Solve issue #123 end-to-end
```

Or step-by-step:

```
/spec 123    # Plan implementation
/exec 123    # Build in isolated worktree
/qa 123      # Review before merge
```

### Prerequisites

**Required (one of):**
- [Claude Code](https://claude.ai/code) — default agent
- [Aider](https://aider.chat/) — alternative via `--agent aider`
- [GitHub CLI](https://cli.github.com/) — run `gh auth login`
- Git (for worktree-based isolation)

**For npm installation:**
- Node.js 20+

**Optional MCP servers (enhanced features):**
- `chrome-devtools` — enables `/test` for browser-based UI testing
- `sequential-thinking` — enhanced reasoning for complex decisions
- `context7` — library documentation lookup

content.content:
# What Is Sequant

**Core Definition:**
"Sequant is workflow automation for Claude Code. It turns GitHub issues into merge-ready pull requests through structured phases and quality gates."

## What Sequant Does

Sequant automates the development workflow by transforming unstructured GitHub issues into production-ready code. The system guides users through a disciplined process rather than ad-hoc implementation.

**Key Capabilities:**

The tool enforces a phased workflow with three main stages:

1. **Planning** (`/spec`): Analyzes issues in the main repository, validates acceptance criteria, and documents the plan on GitHub

2. **Building** (`/exec`): Implements features in an isolated worktree (separate branch with dependencies), creates commits, and opens pull requests

3. **Review** (`/qa`): Runs quality checks covering type safety, security, scope validation, and test coverage—with automatic fixes available through `/loop` (up to 3 iterations)

**Execution Options:**

Users can run individual commands or consolidate the entire process with `/fullsolve 123` (where 123 is the issue number).

## The Problem It Solves

Sequant addresses inconsistencies in AI-assisted development by adding structure. Without formal processes, issues produce variable results—vague implementations, missing review steps, accumulated context drift, and lost visibility on project status.

Human development relies on methodologies, code review, CI/CD, and project management. Sequant brings equivalent discipline to Claude Code workflows.

1	# What Is Sequant
2	
3	Sequant is workflow automation for Claude Code. It turns GitHub issues into
4	merge-ready pull requests through structured phases and quality gates.
5	
6	You write issues. Sequant plans, builds, tests, and reviews them — each step
7	with guardrails that catch problems before they reach your codebase.
8	
9	---
10	
11	## The Problem
12	
13	Claude Code is remarkably capable. Give it a well-scoped issue and it can
14	plan, implement, and test a feature in minutes. But capability alone doesn't
15	guarantee consistency.
16	
17	Without structure, AI-assisted development has a "results may vary" quality:
18	
19	- A vague issue produces a vague implementation
20	- There's no formal review step — code goes from "written" to "done"
21	- Context accumulates over long sessions, drifting from the original goal
22	- There's nothing tracking which issues are planned, in progress, or reviewed
23	- Each session starts from scratch with no memory of prior work
24	
25	These aren't flaws in Claude Code. They're the natural consequence of
26	working without a process. Human developers solve this with methodologies,
27	code review, CI/CD, and project management. AI-assisted development needs
28	the same discipline.
29	
30	Sequant provides that discipline.
31	
32	---
33	
34	## How It Works
35	
36	Sequant installs slash commands into Claude Code that enforce a phased
37	workflow. Each issue moves through plan, build, and review — with an
38	isolated worktree keeping your main branch clean and a quality loop
39	that catches problems before merge.
40	
41	```text
42	                         ┌──────────────┐
43	                         │ GitHub Issue │
44	                         │    #123      │
45	                         └──────┬───────┘
46	                                │
47	                         ┌──────▼───────┐
48	                         │    /spec     │  Runs in main repo.
49	                         │     Plan     │  Reads issue, lints AC,
50	                         │              │  posts plan to GitHub.
51	                         └──────┬───────┘
52	                                │
53	               ┌────────────────▼──────────────────────┐
54	               │           WORKTREE                    │
55	               │    isolated branch + dependencies     │
56	               │                                       │
57	               │         ┌──────────────┐              │
58	               │         │    /exec     │              │
59	               │         │    Build     │              │
60	               │         │              │              │
61	               │         │ Implement,   │              │
62	               │         │ commit, push,│              │
63	               │         │ create PR    │              │
64	               │         └──────┬───────┘              │
65	               │                │                      │
66	               │         ┌──────▼───────┐              │
67	               │         │     /qa      │◄──┐          │
68	               │         │    Review    │   │          │
69	               │         │              │   │          │
70	               │         │ Type safety, │   │ /loop    │
71	               │         │ security,    │   │ auto-fix │
72	               │         │ scope, tests │   │ (up to   │
73	               │         └──────┬───────┘   │  3x)     │
74	               │                │           │          │
75	               │           Pass? ───NO──────┘          │
76	               │                │                      │
77	               │               YES                     │
78	               │                │                      │
79	               └────────────────┼──────────────────────┘
80	                                │
81	                         ┌──────▼───────┐
82	                         │    Merge     │  PR lands on main.
83	                         │   + Clean    │  Worktree removed.
84	                         └──────────────┘
85	                                │
86	                                ▼
87	                         Code on main
88	```
89	
90	Or run the full pipeline in one command:
91	
92	```text
93	/fullsolve 123
94	```
95	
96	### Each Phase Is a Fresh Conversation
97	
98	This is a deliberate design choice. Phases don't share implicit context.
99	The QA phase reviews the actual code diff — not a memory of having written
100	it. The implementation phase reads the spec plan from the issue comment —
101	not from conversation history.
102	
103	Cross-phase context flows through explicit, inspectable channels:
104	
105	| Channel | What it carries |
106	| --- | --- |
107	| **GitHub Comments** | Spec plans, QA verdicts, and phase markers posted to the issue thread |
108	| **Git Diff** | What actually changed in the worktree |
109	| **State File** (`.sequant/`) | Phase progress, AC status, and timestamps tracked locally per issue |
110	| **Environment** | Issue number, worktree path, and base branch passed between phases |
111	
112	This prevents context pollution, keeps phases composable, and makes the
113	entire workflow auditable by reading issue comments.
114	
115	---
116	
117	## What Sequant Installs
118	
119	Sequant is not a platform or a service. It's a set of files that live
120	in your project and extend Claude Code's capabilities.
121	
122	```text
123	your-project/
124	│
125	├── .claude/
126	│   ├── skills/              ◄── 18 slash commands (markdown prompts)
127	│   │   ├── spec/SKILL.md         /spec, /exec, /qa, /fullsolve,
128	│   │   ├── exec/SKILL.md         /test, /testgen, /loop, /assess,
129	│   │   ├── qa/SKILL.md           /verify, /docs, /clean,
130	│   │   └── ...                   /improve, /reflect, /merger,
131	│   │                             /security-review, /upstream
132	│   ├── hooks/               ◄── 2 Claude Code hooks
133	│   │   ├── pre-tool.sh           Security guardrails, timing
134	│   │   └── post-tool.sh          Quality observability, formatting
135	│   └── settings.json        ◄── Hook configuration
136	│
137	├── .sequant/
138	│   ├── state.json           ◄── Workflow state per issue
139	│   ├── settings.json        ◄── Project configuration
140	│   ├── metrics.json         ◄── Aggregated run analytics
141	│   └── logs/                ◄── Structured JSON run logs
142	│
143	├── scripts/dev/
144	│   ├── new-feature.sh       ◄── Create worktree from issue
145	│   ├── cleanup-worktree.sh  ◄── Remove worktree after merge
146	│   └── list-worktrees.sh    ◄── Show active worktrees
147	│
148	└── .sequant-manifest.json   ◄── Version tracking for updates
149	```
150	
151	Everything is local. No telemetry. No external services. Your code and
152	workflow data stay in your repository.
153	
154	---
155	
156	## What the Quality Gates Catch
157	
158	The `/qa` phase isn't a rubber stamp. It runs structured checks against
159	your code and renders one of four verdicts:
160	
161	| Verdict | Meaning |
162	| ------- | ------- |
163	| **READY_FOR_MERGE** | All acceptance criteria met, quality checks pass |
164	| **AC_MET_BUT_NOT_A_PLUS** | Functional but has suggestions for improvement |
165	| **NEEDS_VERIFICATION** | Requires manual testing or confirmation |
166	| **AC_NOT_MET** | Acceptance criteria not satisfied — needs fixes |
167	
168	The checks behind those verdicts:
169	
170	```text
171	┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
172	│                     /qa Quality Gates                   │
173	├─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
174	│                                                         │
175	│  AC Adherence ─── Does the code do what the issue asked │
176	│                                                         │
177	│  Type Safety ──── Detects `any`, missing types,         │
178	│                   unsafe casts                          │
179	│                                                         │
180	│  Security ─────── Semgrep static analysis, OWASP        │
181	│                   checks, secret detection              │
182	│                                                         │
183	│  Scope ────────── Flags changes outside issue scope     │
184	│                                                         │

# What Is Sequant

Sequant is workflow automation for Claude Code. It turns GitHub issues into
merge-ready pull requests through structured phases and quality gates.

You write issues. Sequant plans, builds, tests, and reviews them — each step
with guardrails that catch problems before they reach your codebase.

---

## The Problem

Claude Code is remarkably capable. Give it a well-scoped issue and it can
plan, implement, and test a feature in minutes. But capability alone doesn't
guarantee consistency.

Without structure, AI-assisted development has a "results may vary" quality:

- A vague issue produces a vague implementation
- There's no formal review step — code goes from "written" to "done"
- Context accumulates over long sessions, drifting from the original goal
- There's nothing tracking which issues are planned, in progress, or reviewed
- Each session starts from scratch with no memory of prior work

These aren't flaws in Claude Code. They're the natural consequence of
working without a process. Human developers solve this with methodologies,
code review, CI/CD, and project management. AI-assisted development needs
the same discipline.

Sequant provides that discipline.

---

## How It Works

Sequant installs slash commands into Claude Code that enforce a phased
workflow. Each issue moves through plan, build, and review — with an
isolated worktree keeping your main branch clean and a quality loop
that catches problems before merge.

```text
                         ┌──────────────┐
                         │ GitHub Issue │
                         │    #123      │
                         └──────┬───────┘
                                │
                         ┌──────▼───────┐
                         │    /spec     │  Runs in main repo.
                         │     Plan     │  Reads issue, lints AC,
                         │              │  posts plan to GitHub.
                         └──────┬───────┘
                                │
               ┌────────────────▼──────────────────────┐
               │           WORKTREE                    │
               │    isolated branch + dependencies     │
               │                                       │
               │         ┌──────────────┐              │
               │         │    /exec     │              │
               │         │    Build     │              │
               │         │              │              │
               │         │ Implement,   │              │
               │         │ commit, push,│              │
               │         │ create PR    │              │
               │         └──────┬───────┘              │
               │                │                      │
               │         ┌──────▼───────┐              │
               │         │     /qa      │◄──┐          │
               │         │    Review    │   │          │
               │         │              │   │          │
               │         │ Type safety, │   │ /loop    │
               │         │ security,    │   │ auto-fix │
               │         │ scope, tests │   │ (up to   │
               │         └──────┬───────┘   │  3x)     │
               │                │           │          │
               │           Pass? ───NO──────┘          │
               │                │                      │
               │               YES                     │
               │                │                      │
               └────────────────┼──────────────────────┘
                                │
                         ┌──────▼───────┐
                         │    Merge     │  PR lands on main.
                         │   + Clean    │  Worktree removed.
                         └──────────────┘
                                │
                                ▼
                         Code on main
```

Or run the full pipeline in one command:

```text
/fullsolve 123
```

### Each Phase Is a Fresh Conversation

This is a deliberate design choice. Phases don't share implicit context.
The QA phase reviews the actual code diff — not a memory of having written
it. The implementation phase reads the spec plan from the issue comment —
not from conversation history.

Cross-phase context flows through explicit, inspectable channels:

| Channel | What it carries |
| --- | --- |
| **GitHub Comments** | Spec plans, QA verdicts, and phase markers posted to the issue thread |
| **Git Diff** | What actually changed in the worktree |
| **State File** (`.sequant/`) | Phase progress, AC status, and timestamps tracked locally per issue |
| **Environment** | Issue number, worktree path, and base branch passed between phases |

This prevents context pollution, keeps phases composable, and makes the
entire workflow auditable by reading issue comments.

---

## What Sequant Installs

Sequant is not a platform or a service. It's a set of files that live
in your project and extend Claude Code's capabilities.

```text
your-project/
│
├── .claude/
│   ├── skills/              ◄── 18 slash commands (markdown prompts)
│   │   ├── spec/SKILL.md         /spec, /exec, /qa, /fullsolve,
│   │   ├── exec/SKILL.md         /test, /testgen, /loop, /assess,
│   │   ├── qa/SKILL.md           /verify, /docs, /clean,
│   │   └── ...                   /improve, /reflect, /merger,
│   │                             /security-review, /upstream
│   ├── hooks/               ◄── 2 Claude Code hooks
│   │   ├── pre-tool.sh           Security guardrails, timing
│   │   └── post-tool.sh          Quality observability, formatting
│   └── settings.json        ◄── Hook configuration
│
├── .sequant/
│   ├── state.json           ◄── Workflow state per issue
│   ├── settings.json        ◄── Project configuration
│   ├── metrics.json         ◄── Aggregated run analytics
│   └── logs/                ◄── Structured JSON run logs
│
├── scripts/dev/
│   ├── new-feature.sh       ◄── Create worktree from issue
│   ├── cleanup-worktree.sh  ◄── Remove worktree after merge
│   └── list-worktrees.sh    ◄── Show active worktrees
│
└── .sequant-manifest.json   ◄── Version tracking for updates
```

Everything is local. No telemetry. No external services. Your code and
workflow data stay in your repository.

---

## What the Quality Gates Catch

The `/qa` phase isn't a rubber stamp. It runs structured checks against
your code and renders one of four verdicts:

| Verdict | Meaning |
| ------- | ------- |
| **READY_FOR_MERGE** | All acceptance criteria met, quality checks pass |
| **AC_MET_BUT_NOT_A_PLUS** | Functional but has suggestions for improvement |
| **NEEDS_VERIFICATION** | Requires manual testing or confirmation |
| **AC_NOT_MET** | Acceptance criteria not satisfied — needs fixes |

The checks behind those verdicts:

```text
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│                     /qa Quality Gates                   │
├─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│                                                         │
│  AC Adherence ─── Does the code do what the issue asked │
│                                                         │
│  Type Safety ──── Detects `any`, missing types,         │
│                   unsafe casts                          │
│                                                         │
│  Security ─────── Semgrep static analysis, OWASP        │
│                   checks, secret detection              │
│                                                         │
│  Scope ────────── Flags changes outside issue scope     │
│                                                         │
│  Test Coverage ── File-by-file coverage, critical path  │
│                   flagging, deleted test detection      │
│                                                         │
│  Build ────────── Distinguishes regressions from        │
│                   pre-existing failures                 │
│                                                         │
│  Anti-Patterns ── N+1 queries, empty catch blocks,      │
│                   over-mocking, stale dependencies      │
│                                                         │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
```

When checks fail, `/loop` can automatically parse findings, apply fixes,
and re-run — up to 3 iterations — without human intervention.

---

## What Runs Underneath

The phases are the visible workflow. Underneath, four systems run
continuously to keep everything safe, tracked, and connected:

```text
  ┌───────────┐  ┌───────────┐  ┌───────────┐  ┌────────────┐
  │  Skills   │  │   Hooks   │  │   State   │  │   GitHub   │
  │           │  │           │  │           │  │  Comments  │
  │ 18 slash  │  │ Pre-tool: │  │ .sequant/ │  │            │
  │ commands  │  │ blocks    │  │ tracks    │  │ Plans,     │
  │ that tell │  │ secrets,  │  │ phase     │  │ verdicts,  │
  │ Claude    │  │ unsafe    │  │ progress, │  │ and phase  │
  │ Code what │  │ commands  │  │ metrics,  │  │ markers    │
  │ to do in  │  │           │  │ run logs  │  │ that carry │
  │ each      │  │ Post-tool:│  │           │  │ context    │
  │ phase     │  │ formats,  │  │ Updated   │  │ between    │
  │           │  │ observes  │  │ at phase  │  │ sessions   │
  │           │  │ quality   │  │ boundaries│  │            │
  └───────────┘  └───────────┘  └───────────┘  └────────────┘
```

- **Skills** are markdown prompts — they tell Claude Code exactly what to
  do in each phase. The intelligence lives in the prompts.
- **Hooks** fire on every tool call. Pre-tool hooks block secrets,
  destructive commands, and edits outside the worktree. Post-tool hooks
  auto-format code and log quality signals.
- **State** tracks phase progress locally in `.sequant/state.json`,
  aggregates metrics across runs, and writes structured JSON logs.
- **GitHub Comments** carry context between phases. The spec plan, QA
  verdict, and phase markers are all posted to the issue thread —
  making the workflow resumable across sessions and machines.

---

## Worktree Isolation

The worktree in the diagram above isn't just a feature — it's the
boundary between planning and execution. `/spec` runs in your main
repo (no code changes). Everything inside the worktree — `/exec`,
`/qa`, `/loop` — operates on an isolated branch with its own
dependencies. Your main branch is never touched.

```text
your-project/                     ◄── Main repo (stays on main)

../worktrees/feature/
    ├── 123-add-auth/             ◄── Issue #123 (own branch + deps)
    ├── 124-fix-nav-bug/          ◄── Issue #124 (own branch + deps)
    └── 125-update-api/           ◄── Issue #125 (own branch + deps)
```

Work on multiple issues in parallel. Discard failed experiments. When
a PR merges, the worktree is cleaned up and the code lands on main.

---

## Two Ways to Use It

### Interactive — Inside Claude Code

Type slash commands directly. Review plans before building. Step through
at your own pace.

```text
/spec 123          Plan the implementation
/exec 123          Build it
/qa 123            Review it
```

### Headless — From the CLI

Run the full pipeline without the Claude Code UI. Batch multiple issues.
Let it iterate overnight.

```bash
npx sequant run 123                    # Single issue, full pipeline
npx sequant run 1 2 3                  # Three issues in parallel
npx sequant run 123 --quality-loop     # Auto-fix on QA failure
npx sequant run 123 --resume           # Pick up where you left off
```

---

## Issues as Building Blocks

The core idea behind Sequant is that a project is a collection of
well-defined issues, and each issue follows the same lifecycle:

```text
              ┌───────────────────────────────────────────┐
              │              Your Project                 │
              │                                           │
              │   ┌───────┐  ┌───────┐  ┌───────┐         │
              │   │ #101  │  │ #102  │  │ #103  │         │
              │   │ Auth  │  │ Nav   │  │ API   │  ...    │
              │   │ [done]│  │ [done]│  │ [wip] │         │
              │   └───────┘  └───────┘  └───────┘         │
              │                                           │
              │   ┌───────┐  ┌───────┐  ┌───────┐         │
              │   │ #104  │  │ #105  │  │ #106  │         │
              │   │ Tests │  │ Perf  │  │ Docs  │  ...    │
              │   │ [plan]│  │ [plan]│  │ [plan]│         │
              │   └───────┘  └───────┘  └───────┘         │
              │                                           │
              └───────────────────────────────────────────┘
```

Each block passes through the same phases. Each block gets the same
quality checks. The output is predictable because the process is
consistent — not because every issue is simple, but because every
issue is treated with the same rigor.

Sequant doesn't make Claude Code less flexible. It makes the workflow
around it more structured. You still get the full power of Claude Code
inside each phase. Sequant just ensures that power is applied
methodically — plan first, build in isolation, review before merging.

---

## Quick Start

```bash
npx sequant init
npx sequant doctor
```

Then in Claude Code:

```text

```

See the [Quickstart Guide](../guides/quickstart.md) for the full walkthrough.

# Aider Agent Backend

Run Sequant workflows using [Aider](https://aider.chat) instead of Claude Code. Aider is model-agnostic — use Claude, GPT-4o, Gemini, DeepSeek, or local models for phase execution.

## Prerequisites

1. **Aider CLI** — `pip install aider-chat` (verify: `aider --version`)
2. **Model API key** — Set the appropriate env var for your model (e.g., `ANTHROPIC_API_KEY`, `OPENAI_API_KEY`)
3. **Sequant** — `npx sequant doctor` should pass

## Setup

### Option 1: CLI flag (per-run)

```bash
npx sequant run 123 --agent aider
```

### Option 2: Default in settings (permanent)

Add to `.sequant/settings.json`:

```json
{
  "run": {
    "agent": "aider"
  }
}
```

# What Is Sequant

Sequant is workflow automation for Claude Code. It turns GitHub issues into
merge-ready pull requests through structured phases and quality gates.

You write issues. Sequant plans, builds, tests, and reviews them — each step
with guardrails that catch problems before they reach your codebase.

---

## The Problem

Claude Code is remarkably capable. Give it a well-scoped issue and it can
plan, implement, and test a feature in minutes. But capability alone doesn't
guarantee consistency.

Without structure, AI-assisted development has a "results may vary" quality:

- A vague issue produces a vague implementation
- There's no formal review step — code goes from "written" to "done"
- Context accumulates over long sessions, drifting from the original goal
- There's nothing tracking which issues are planned, in progress, or reviewed
- Each session starts from scratch with no memory of prior work

These aren't flaws in Claude Code. They're the natural consequence of
working without a process. Human developers solve this with methodologies,
code review, CI/CD, and project management. AI-assisted development needs
the same discipline.

Sequant provides that discipline.

---

## How It Works

Sequant installs slash commands into Claude Code that enforce a phased
workflow. Each issue moves through plan, build, and review — with an
isolated worktree keeping your main branch clean and a quality loop
that catches problems before merge.

```text
                         ┌──────────────┐
                         │ GitHub Issue │
                         │    #123      │
                         └──────┬───────┘
                                │
                         ┌──────▼───────┐
                         │    /spec     │  Runs in main repo.
                         │     Plan     │  Reads issue, lints AC,
                         │              │  posts plan to GitHub.
                         └──────┬───────┘
                                │
               ┌────────────────▼──────────────────────┐
               │           WORKTREE                    │
               │    isolated branch + dependencies     │
               │                                       │
               │         ┌──────────────┐              │
               │         │    /exec     │              │
               │         │    Build     │              │
               │         │              │              │
               │         │ Implement,   │              │
               │         │ commit, push,│              │
               │         │ create PR    │              │
               │         └──────┬───────┘              │
               │                │                      │
               │         ┌──────▼───────┐              │
               │         │     /qa      │◄──┐          │
               │         │    Review    │   │          │
               │         │              │   │          │
               │         │ Type safety, │   │ /loop    │
               │         │ security,    │   │ auto-fix │
               │         │ scope, tests │   │ (up to   │
               │         └──────┬───────┘   │  3x)     │
               │                │           │          │
               │           Pass? ───NO──────┘          │
               │                │                      │
               │               YES                     │
               │                │                      │
               └────────────────┼──────────────────────┘
                                │
                         ┌──────▼───────┐
                         │    Merge     │  PR lands on main.
                         │   + Clean    │  Worktree removed.
                         └──────────────┘
                                │
                                ▼
                         Code on main
```

Or run the full pipeline in one command:

```text
/fullsolve 123
```

### Each Phase Is a Fresh Conversation

This is a deliberate design choice. Phases don't share implicit context.
The QA phase reviews the actual code diff — not a memory of having written
it. The implementation phase reads the spec plan from the issue comment —
not from conversation history.

Cross-phase context flows through explicit, inspectable channels:

| Channel | What it carries |
| --- | --- |
| **GitHub Comments** | Spec plans, QA verdicts, and phase markers posted to the issue thread |
| **Git Diff** | What actually changed in the worktree |
| **State File** (`.sequant/`) | Phase progress, AC status, and timestamps tracked locally per issue |
| **Environment** | Issue number, worktree path, and base branch passed between phases |

This prevents context pollution, keeps phases composable, and makes the
entire workflow auditable by reading issue comments.

---

## What Sequant Installs

Sequant is not a platform or a service. It's a set of files that live
in your project and extend Claude Code's capabilities.

```text
your-project/
│
├── .claude/
│   ├── skills/              ◄── 18 slash commands (markdown prompts)
│   │   ├── spec/SKILL.md         /spec, /exec, /qa, /fullsolve,
│   │   ├── exec/SKILL.md         /test, /testgen, /loop, /assess,
│   │   ├── qa/SKILL.md           /verify, /docs, /clean,
│   │   └── ...                   /improve, /reflect, /merger,
│   │                             /security-review, /upstream
│   ├── hooks/               ◄── 2 Claude Code hooks
│   │   ├── pre-tool.sh           Security guardrails, timing
│   │   └── post-tool.sh          Quality observability, formatting
│   └── settings.json        ◄── Hook configuration
│
├── .sequant/
│   ├── state.json           ◄── Workflow state per issue
│   ├── settings.json        ◄── Project configuration
│   ├── metrics.json         ◄── Aggregated run analytics
│   └── logs/                ◄── Structured JSON run logs
│
├── scripts/dev/
│   ├── new-feature.sh       ◄── Create worktree from issue
│   ├── cleanup-worktree.sh  ◄── Remove worktree after merge
│   └── list-worktrees.sh    ◄── Show active worktrees
│
└── .sequant-manifest.json   ◄── Version tracking for updates
```

Everything is local. No telemetry. No external services. Your code and
workflow data stay in your repository.

---

## What the Quality Gates Catch

The `/qa` phase isn't a rubber stamp. It runs structured checks against
your code and renders one of four verdicts:

| Verdict | Meaning |
| ------- | ------- |
| **READY_FOR_MERGE** | All acceptance criteria met, quality checks pass |
| **AC_MET_BUT_NOT_A_PLUS** | Functional but has suggestions for improvement |
| **NEEDS_VERIFICATION** | Requires manual testing or confirmation |
| **AC_NOT_MET** | Acceptance criteria not satisfied — needs fixes |

The checks behind those verdicts:

```text
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│                     /qa Quality Gates                   │
├─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│                                                         │
│  AC Adherence ─── Does the code do what the issue asked │
│                                                         │
│  Type Safety ──── Detects `any`, missing types,         │
│                   unsafe casts                          │
│                                                         │
│  Security ─────── Semgrep static analysis, OWASP        │
│                   checks, secret detection              │
│                                                         │
│  Scope ────────── Flags changes outside issue scope     │
│                                                         │
│  Test Coverage ── File-by-file coverage, critical path  │
│                   flagging, deleted test detection      │
│                                                         │
│  Build ────────── Distinguishes regressions from        │
│                   pre-existing failures                 │
│                                                         │
│  Anti-Patterns ── N+1 queries, empty catch blocks,      │
│                   over-mocking, stale dependencies      │
│                                                         │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
```

When checks fail, `/loop` can automatically parse findings, apply fixes,
and re-run — up to 3 iterations — without human intervention.

---

## What Runs Underneath

The phases are the visible workflow. Underneath, four systems run
continuously to keep everything safe, tracked, and connected:

```text
  ┌───────────┐  ┌───────────┐  ┌───────────┐  ┌────────────┐
  │  Skills   │  │   Hooks   │  │   State   │  │   GitHub   │
  │           │  │           │  │           │  │  Comments  │
  │ 18 slash  │  │ Pre-tool: │  │ .sequant/ │  │            │
  │ commands  │  │ blocks    │  │ tracks    │  │ Plans,     │
  │ that tell │  │ secrets,  │  │ phase     │  │ verdicts,  │
  │ Claude    │  │ unsafe    │  │ progress, │  │ and phase  │
  │ Code what │  │ commands  │  │ metrics,  │  │ markers    │
  │ to do in  │  │           │  │ run logs  │  │ that carry │
  │ each      │  │ Post-tool:│  │           │  │ context    │
  │ phase     │  │ formats,  │  │ Updated   │  │ between    │
  │           │  │ observes  │  │ at phase  │  │ sessions   │
  │           │  │ quality   │  │ boundaries│  │            │
  └───────────┘  └───────────┘  └───────────┘  └────────────┘
```

- **Skills** are markdown prompts — they tell Claude Code exactly what to
  do in each phase. The intelligence lives in the prompts.
- **Hooks** fire on every tool call. Pre-tool hooks block secrets,
  destructive commands, and edits outside the worktree. Post-tool hooks
  auto-format code and log quality signals.
- **State** tracks phase progress locally in `.sequant/state.json`,
  aggregates metrics across runs, and writes structured JSON logs.
- **GitHub Comments** carry context between phases. The spec plan, QA
  verdict, and phase markers are all posted to the issue thread —
  making the workflow resumable across sessions and machines.

---

## Worktree Isolation

The worktree in the diagram above isn't just a feature — it's the
boundary between planning and execution. `/spec` runs in your main
repo (no code changes). Everything inside the worktree — `/exec`,
`/qa`, `/loop` — operates on an isolated branch with its own
dependencies. Your main branch is never touched.

```text
your-project/                     ◄── Main repo (stays on main)

../worktrees/feature/
    ├── 123-add-auth/             ◄── Issue #123 (own branch + deps)
    ├── 124-fix-nav-bug/          ◄── Issue #124 (own branch + deps)
    └── 125-update-api/           ◄── Issue #125 (own branch + deps)
```

Work on multiple issues in parallel. Discard failed experiments. When
a PR merges, the worktree is cleaned up and the code lands on main.

---

## Two Ways to Use It

### Interactive — Inside Claude Code

Type slash commands directly. Review plans before building. Step through
at your own pace.

```text
/spec 123          Plan the implementation
/exec 123          Build it
/qa 123            Review it
```

### Headless — From the CLI

Run the full pipeline without the Claude Code UI. Batch multiple issues.
Let it iterate overnight.

```bash
npx sequant run 123                    # Single issue, full pipeline
npx sequant run 1 2 3                  # Three issues in parallel
npx sequant run 123 --quality-loop     # Auto-fix on QA failure
npx sequant run 123 --resume           # Pick up where you left off
```

---

## Issues as Building Blocks

The core idea behind Sequant is that a project is a collection of
well-defined issues, and each issue follows the same lifecycle:

```text
              ┌───────────────────────────────────────────┐
              │              Your Project                 │
              │                                           │
              │   ┌───────┐  ┌───────┐  ┌───────┐         │
              │   │ #101  │  │ #102  │  │ #103  │         │
              │   │ Auth  │  │ Nav   │  │ API   │  ...    │
              │   │ [done]│  │ [done]│  │ [wip] │         │
              │   └───────┘  └───────┘  └───────┘         │
              │                                           │
              │   ┌───────┐  ┌───────┐  ┌───────┐         │
              │   │ #104  │  │ #105  │  │ #106  │         │
              │   │ Tests │  │ Perf  │  │ Docs  │  ...    │
              │   │ [plan]│  │ [plan]│  │ [plan]│         │
              │   └───────┘  └───────┘  └───────┘         │
              │                                           │
              └───────────────────────────────────────────┘
```

Each block passes through the same phases. Each block gets the same
quality checks. The output is predictable because the process is
consistent — not because every issue is simple, but because every
issue is treated with the same rigor.

Sequant doesn't make Claude Code less flexible. It makes the workflow
around it more structured. You still get the full power of Claude Code
inside each phase. Sequant just ensures that power is applied
methodically — plan first, build in isolation, review before merging.

---

## Quick Start

```bash
npx sequant init
npx sequant doctor
```

Then in Claude Code:

```text
/fullsolve 123
```

See the [Quickstart Guide](../guides/quickstart.md) for the full walkthrough.

- **Skills** are markdown prompts — they tell Claude Code exactly what to
  do in each phase. The intelligence lives in the prompts.
- **Hooks** fire on every tool call. Pre-tool hooks block secrets,
  destructive commands, and edits outside the worktree. Post-tool hooks
  auto-format code and log quality signals.
- **State** tracks phase progress locally in `.sequant/state.json`,
  aggregates metrics across runs, and writes structured JSON logs.
- **GitHub Comments** carry context between phases. The spec plan, QA
  verdict, and phase markers are all posted to the issue thread —
  making the workflow resumable across sessions and machines.

---

## Worktree Isolation

The worktree in the diagram above isn't just a feature — it's the
boundary between planning and execution. `/spec` runs in your main
repo (no code changes). Everything inside the worktree — `/exec`,
`/qa`, `/loop` — operates on an isolated branch with its own
dependencies. Your main branch is never touched.

```

Work on multiple issues in parallel. Discard failed experiments. When
a PR merges, the worktree is cleaned up and the code lands on main.

---

## Two Ways to Use It

### Interactive — Inside Claude Code

Type slash commands directly. Review plans before building. Step through
at your own pace.

```text
/spec 123          Plan the implementation
/exec 123          Build it
/qa 123            Review it
```

### Headless — From the CLI

Run the full pipeline without the Claude Code UI. Batch multiple issues.
Let it iterate overnight.

```bash
npx sequant run 123                    # Single issue, full pipeline
npx sequant run 1 2 3                  # Three issues in parallel
npx sequant run 123 --quality-loop     # Auto-fix on QA failure
npx sequant run 123 --resume           # Pick up where you left off
```

---

## Issues as Building Blocks

The core idea behind Sequant is that a project is a collection of
well-defined issues, and each issue follows the same lifecycle:

```text
              ┌───────────────────────────────────────────┐
              │              Your Project                 │
              │                                           │
              │   ┌───────┐  ┌───────┐  ┌───────┐         │
              │   │ #101  │  │ #102  │  │ #103  │         │
              │   │ Auth  │  │ Nav   │  │ API   │  ...    │
              │   │ [done]│  │ [done]│  │ [wip] │         │
              │   └───────┘  └───────┘  └───────┘         │
              │                                           │
              │   ┌───────┐  ┌───────┐  ┌───────┐         │
              │   │ #104  │  │ #105  │  │ #106  │         │
              │   │ Tests │  │ Perf  │  │ Docs  │  ...    │
              │   │ [plan]│  │ [plan]│  │ [plan]│         │
              │   └───────┘  └───────┘  └───────┘         │
              │                                           │
              └───────────────────────────────────────────┘
```

Each block passes through the same phases. Each block gets the same
quality checks. The output is predictable because the process is
consistent — not because every issue is simple, but because every
issue is treated with the same rigor.

Sequant doesn't make Claude Code less flexible. It makes the workflow
around it more structured. You still get the full power of Claude Code
inside each phase. Sequant just ensures that power is applied
methodically — plan first, build in isolation, review before merging.

---

## Quick Start

```bash
npx sequant init
npx sequant doctor
```

Then in Claude Code:

```text
/fullsolve 123
```

See the [Quickstart Guide](../guides/quickstart.md) for the full walkthrough.

# What Is Sequant

Sequant is workflow automation for AI coding agents. It turns GitHub issues
into merge-ready pull requests through structured phases and quality gates.

You write issues. Sequant plans, builds, tests, and reviews them — each step
with guardrails that catch problems before they reach your codebase.

---

## The Problem

AI coding agents are remarkably capable. Give one a well-scoped issue and it
can plan, implement, and test a feature in minutes. But capability alone
doesn't guarantee consistency.

Without structure, AI-assisted development has a "results may vary" quality:

- A vague issue produces a vague implementation
- There's no formal review step — code goes from "written" to "done"
- Context accumulates over long sessions, drifting from the original goal
- There's nothing tracking which issues are planned, in progress, or reviewed
- Each session starts from scratch with no memory of prior work

These aren't flaws in the agent. They're the natural consequence of working
without a process. Human developers solve this with methodologies, code
review, CI/CD, and project management. AI-assisted development needs the
same discipline.

Sequant provides that discipline.

---

## How It Works

Sequant enforces a phased workflow through slash commands, an MCP server,
and a headless CLI. Each issue moves through plan, build, and review — with
an isolated worktree keeping your main branch clean and a quality loop that
catches problems before merge.

```text
                         ┌──────────────┐
                         │ GitHub Issue │
                         │    #123      │
                         └──────┬───────┘
                                │
                         ┌──────▼───────┐
                         │    /spec     │  Runs in main repo.
                         │     Plan     │  Reads issue, lints AC,
                         │              │  posts plan to GitHub.
                         └──────┬───────┘
                                │
               ┌────────────────▼──────────────────────┐
               │           WORKTREE                    │
               │    isolated branch + dependencies     │
               │                                       │
               │         ┌──────────────┐              │
               │         │    /exec     │              │
               │         │    Build     │              │
               │         │              │              │
               │         │ Implement,   │              │
               │         │ commit, push,│              │
               │         │ create PR    │              │
               │         └──────┬───────┘              │
               │                │                      │
               │         ┌──────▼───────┐              │
               │         │     /qa      │◄──┐          │
               │         │    Review    │   │          │
               │         │              │   │          │
               │         │ Type safety, │   │ /loop    │
               │         │ security,    │   │ auto-fix │
               │         │ scope, tests │   │ (up to   │
               │         └──────┬───────┘   │  3x)     │
               │                │           │          │
               │           Pass? ───NO──────┘          │
               │                │                      │
               │               YES                     │
               │                │                      │
               └────────────────┼──────────────────────┘
                                │
                         ┌──────▼───────┐
                         │    Merge     │  PR lands on main.
                         │   + Clean    │  Worktree removed.
                         └──────────────┘
                                │
                                ▼
                         Code on main
```

Or run the full pipeline in one command:

```text
/fullsolve 123
```

### Each Phase Is a Fresh Conversation

This is a deliberate design choice. Phases don't share implicit context.
The QA phase reviews the actual code diff — not a memory of having written
it. The implementation phase reads the spec plan from the issue comment —
not from conversation history.

Cross-phase context flows through explicit, inspectable channels:

| Channel | What it carries |
| --- | --- |
| **GitHub Comments** | Spec plans, QA verdicts, and phase markers posted to the issue thread |
| **Git Diff** | What actually changed in the worktree |
| **State File** (`.sequant/`) | Phase progress, AC status, and timestamps tracked locally per issue |
| **Environment** | Issue number, worktree path, and base branch passed between phases |

This prevents context pollution, keeps phases composable, and makes the
entire workflow auditable by reading issue comments.

---

## What Sequant Installs

Sequant is not a platform or a service. It's a set of files that live
in your project and extend Claude Code's capabilities.

```text
your-project/
│
├── .claude/
│   ├── skills/              ◄── 18 slash commands (markdown prompts)
│   │   ├── spec/SKILL.md         /spec, /exec, /qa, /fullsolve,
│   │   ├── exec/SKILL.md         /test, /testgen, /loop, /assess,
│   │   ├── qa/SKILL.md           /verify, /docs, /clean,
│   │   └── ...                   /improve, /reflect, /merger,
│   │                             /security-review, /upstream
│   ├── hooks/               ◄── 2 Claude Code hooks
│   │   ├── pre-tool.sh           Security guardrails, timing
│   │   └── post-tool.sh          Quality observability, formatting
│   └── settings.json        ◄── Hook configuration
│
├── .sequant/
│   ├── state.json           ◄── Workflow state per issue
│   ├── settings.json        ◄── Project configuration
│   ├── metrics.json         ◄── Aggregated run analytics
│   └── logs/                ◄── Structured JSON run logs
│
├── scripts/dev/
│   ├── new-feature.sh       ◄── Create worktree from issue
│   ├── cleanup-worktree.sh  ◄── Remove worktree after merge
│   └── list-worktrees.sh    ◄── Show active worktrees
│
└── .sequant-manifest.json   ◄── Version tracking for updates
```

Everything is local. No telemetry. No external services. Your code and
workflow data stay in your repository.

---

## What the Quality Gates Catch

The `/qa` phase isn't a rubber stamp. It runs structured checks against
your code and renders one of four verdicts:

| Verdict | Meaning |
| ------- | ------- |
| **READY_FOR_MERGE** | All acceptance criteria met, quality checks pass |
| **AC_MET_BUT_NOT_A_PLUS** | Functional but has suggestions for improvement |
| **NEEDS_VERIFICATION** | Requires manual testing or confirmation |
| **AC_NOT_MET** | Acceptance criteria not satisfied — needs fixes |

The checks behind those verdicts:

```text
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│                     /qa Quality Gates                   │
├─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│                                                         │
│  AC Adherence ─── Does the code do what the issue asked │
│                                                         │
│  Type Safety ──── Detects `any`, missing types,         │
│                   unsafe casts                          │
│                                                         │
│  Security ─────── Semgrep static analysis, OWASP        │
│                   checks, secret detection              │
│                                                         │
│  Scope ────────── Flags changes outside issue scope     │
│                                                         │
│  Test Coverage ── File-by-file coverage, critical path  │
│                   flagging, deleted test detection      │
│                                                         │
│  Build ────────── Distinguishes regressions from        │
│                   pre-existing failures                 │
│                                                         │
│  Anti-Patterns ── N+1 queries, empty catch blocks,      │
│                   over-mocking, stale dependencies      │
│                                                         │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
```

When checks fail, `/loop` can automatically parse findings, apply fixes,
and re-run — up to 3 iterations — without human intervention.

toolUseResult.originalFile:
# What Is Sequant

Sequant is workflow automation for AI coding agents. It turns GitHub issues
into merge-ready pull requests through structured phases and quality gates.

You write issues. Sequant plans, builds, tests, and reviews them — each step
with guardrails that catch problems before they reach your codebase.

---

## The Problem

AI coding agents are remarkably capable. Give one a well-scoped issue and it
can plan, implement, and test a feature in minutes. But capability alone
doesn't guarantee consistency.

Without structure, AI-assisted development has a "results may vary" quality:

- A vague issue produces a vague implementation
- There's no formal review step — code goes from "written" to "done"
- Context accumulates over long sessions, drifting from the original goal
- There's nothing tracking which issues are planned, in progress, or reviewed
- Each session starts from scratch with no memory of prior work

These aren't flaws in the agent. They're the natural consequence of working
without a process. Human developers solve this with methodologies, code
review, CI/CD, and project management. AI-assisted development needs the
same discipline.

Sequant provides that discipline.

---

## How It Works

Sequant enforces a phased workflow through slash commands, an MCP server,
and a headless CLI. Each issue moves through plan, build, and review — with
an isolated worktree keeping your main branch clean and a quality loop that
catches problems before merge.

```text
                         ┌──────────────┐
                         │ GitHub Issue │
                         │    #123      │
                         └──────┬───────┘
                                │
                         ┌──────▼───────┐
                         │    /spec     │  Runs in main repo.
                         │     Plan     │  Reads issue, lints AC,
                         │              │  posts plan to GitHub.
                         └──────┬───────┘
                                │
               ┌────────────────▼──────────────────────┐
               │           WORKTREE                    │
               │    isolated branch + dependencies     │
               │                                       │
               │         ┌──────────────┐              │
               │         │    /exec     │              │
               │         │    Build     │              │
               │         │              │              │
               │         │ Implement,   │              │
               │         │ commit, push,│              │
               │         │ create PR    │              │
               │         └──────┬───────┘              │
               │                │                      │
               │         ┌──────▼───────┐              │
               │         │     /qa      │◄──┐          │
               │         │    Review    │   │          │
               │         │              │   │          │
               │         │ Type safety, │   │ /loop    │
               │         │ security,    │   │ auto-fix │
               │         │ scope, tests │   │ (up to   │
               │         └──────┬───────┘   │  3x)     │
               │                │           │          │
               │           Pass? ───NO──────┘          │
               │                │                      │
               │               YES                     │
               │                │                      │
               └────────────────┼──────────────────────┘
                                │
                         ┌──────▼───────┐
                         │    Merge     │  PR lands on main.
                         │   + Clean    │  Worktree removed.
                         └──────────────┘
                                │
                                ▼
                         Code on main
```

Or run the full pipeline in one command:

```text
/fullsolve 123
```

### Each Phase Is a Fresh Conversation

This is a deliberate design choice. Phases don't share implicit context.
The QA phase reviews the actual code diff — not a memory of having written
it. The implementation phase reads the spec plan from the issue comment —
not from conversation history.

Cross-phase context flows through explicit, inspectable channels:

| Channel | What it carries |
| --- | --- |
| **GitHub Comments** | Spec plans, QA verdicts, and phase markers posted to the issue thread |
| **Git Diff** | What actually changed in the worktree |
| **State File** (`.sequant/`) | Phase progress, AC status, and timestamps tracked locally per issue |
| **Environment** | Issue number, worktree path, and base branch passed between phases |

This prevents context pollution, keeps phases composable, and makes the
entire workflow auditable by reading issue comments.

---

## What Sequant Installs

Sequant is not a platform or a service. It's a set of files that live
in your project.

```text
your-project/
│
├── .claude/
│   ├── skills/              ◄── 18 slash commands (markdown prompts)
│   │   ├── spec/SKILL.md         /spec, /exec, /qa, /fullsolve,
│   │   ├── exec/SKILL.md         /test, /testgen, /loop, /assess,
│   │   ├── qa/SKILL.md           /verify, /docs, /clean,
│   │   └── ...                   /improve, /reflect, /merger,
│   │                             /security-review, /upstream
│   ├── hooks/               ◄── 2 Claude Code hooks
│   │   ├── pre-tool.sh           Security guardrails, timing
│   │   └── post-tool.sh          Quality observability, formatting
│   └── settings.json        ◄── Hook configuration
│
├── .mcp.json                ◄── MCP server config (for MCP clients)
│
├── .sequant/
│   ├── state.json           ◄── Workflow state per issue
│   ├── settings.json        ◄── Project configuration
│   ├── metrics.json         ◄── Aggregated run analytics
│   └── logs/                ◄── Structured JSON run logs
│
├── scripts/dev/
│   ├── new-feature.sh       ◄── Create worktree from issue
│   ├── cleanup-worktree.sh  ◄── Remove worktree after merge
│   └── list-worktrees.sh    ◄── Show active worktrees
│
└── .sequant-manifest.json   ◄── Version tracking for updates
```

Everything is local. No telemetry. No external services. Your code and
workflow data stay in your repository.

---

## What the Quality Gates Catch

The `/qa` phase isn't a rubber stamp. It runs structured checks against
your code and renders one of four verdicts:

| Verdict | Meaning |
| ------- | ------- |
| **READY_FOR_MERGE** | All acceptance criteria met, quality checks pass |
| **AC_MET_BUT_NOT_A_PLUS** | Functional but has suggestions for improvement |
| **NEEDS_VERIFICATION** | Requires manual testing or confirmation |
| **AC_NOT_MET** | Acceptance criteria not satisfied — needs fixes |

The checks behind those verdicts:

```text
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│                     /qa Quality Gates                   │
├─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│                                                         │
│  AC Adherence ─── Does the code do what the issue asked │
│                                                         │
│  Type Safety ──── Detects `any`, missing types,         │
│                   unsafe casts                          │
│                                                         │
│  Security ─────── Semgrep static analysis, OWASP        │
│                   checks, secret detection              │
│                                                         │
│  Scope ────────── Flags changes outside issue scope     │
│                                                         │
│  Test Coverage ── File-by-file coverage, critical path  │
│                   flagging, deleted test detection      │
│                                                         │
│  Build ────────── Distinguishes regressions from        │
│                   pre-existing failures                 │
│                                                         │
│  Anti-Patterns ── N+1 queries, empty catch blocks,      │
│                   over-mocking, stale dependencies      │
│                                                         │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
```

When checks fail, `/loop` can automatically parse findings, apply fixes,
and re-run — up to 3 iterations — without human intervention.

---

## What Runs Underneath

The phases are the visible workflow. Underneath, four systems run
continuously to keep everything safe, tracked, and connected:

```text
  ┌───────────┐  ┌───────────┐  ┌───────────┐  ┌────────────┐
  │  Skills   │  │   Hooks   │  │   State   │  │   GitHub   │
  │           │  │           │  │           │  │  Comments  │
  │ 18 slash  │  │ Pre-tool: │  │ .sequant/ │  │            │
  │ commands  │  │ blocks    │  │ tracks    │  │ Plans,     │
  │ that tell │  │ secrets,  │  │ phase     │  │ verdicts,  │
  │ Claude    │  │ unsafe    │  │ progress, │  │ and phase  │
  │ Code what │  │ commands  │  │ metrics,  │  │ markers    │
  │ to do in  │  │           │  │ run logs  │  │ that carry │
  │ each      │  │ Post-tool:│  │           │  │ context    │
  │ phase     │  │ formats,  │  │ Updated   │  │ between    │
  │           │  │ observes  │  │ at phase  │  │ sessions   │
  │           │  │ quality   │  │ boundaries│  │            │
  └───────────┘  └───────────┘  └───────────┘  └────────────┘
```

- **Skills** are markdown prompts — they tell Claude Code exactly what to
  do in each phase. The intelligence lives in the prompts.
- **Hooks** fire on every tool call. Pre-tool hooks block secrets,
  destructive commands, and edits outside the worktree. Post-tool hooks
  auto-format code and log quality signals.
- **State** tracks phase progress locally in `.sequant/state.json`,
  aggregates metrics across runs, and writes structured JSON logs.
- **GitHub Comments** carry context between phases. The spec plan, QA
  verdict, and phase markers are all posted to the issue thread —
  making the workflow resumable across sessions and machines.

---

## Worktree Isolation

The worktree in the diagram above isn't just a feature — it's the
boundary between planning and execution. `/spec` runs in your main
repo (no code changes). Everything inside the worktree — `/exec`,
`/qa`, `/loop` — operates on an isolated branch with its own
dependencies. Your main branch is never touched.

```text
your-project/                     ◄── Main repo (stays on main)

../worktrees/feature/
    ├── 123-add-auth/             ◄── Issue #123 (own branch + deps)
    ├── 124-fix-nav-bug/          ◄── Issue #124 (own branch + deps)
    └── 125-update-api/           ◄── Issue #125 (own branch + deps)
```

Work on multiple issues in parallel. Discard failed experiments. When
a PR merges, the worktree is cleaned up and the code lands on main.

---

## Two Ways to Use It

### Interactive — Inside Claude Code

Type slash commands directly. Review plans before building. Step through
at your own pace.

```text
/spec 123          Plan the implementation
/exec 123          Build it
/qa 123            Review it
```

### Headless — From the CLI

Run the full pipeline without the Claude Code UI. Batch multiple issues.
Let it iterate overnight.

```bash
npx sequant run 123                    # Single issue, full pipeline
npx sequant run 1 2 3                  # Three issues in parallel
npx sequant run 123 --quality-loop     # Auto-fix on QA failure
npx sequant run 123 --resume           # Pick up where you left off
```

---

## Issues as Building Blocks

The core idea behind Sequant is that a project is a collection of
well-defined issues, and each issue follows the same lifecycle:

```text
              ┌───────────────────────────────────────────┐
              │              Your Project                 │
              │                                           │
              │   ┌───────┐  ┌───────┐  ┌───────┐         │
              │   │ #101  │  │ #102  │  │ #103  │         │
              │   │ Auth  │  │ Nav   │  │ API   │  ...    │
              │   │ [done]│  │ [done]│  │ [wip] │         │
              │   └───────┘  └───────┘  └───────┘         │
              │                                           │
              │   ┌───────┐  ┌───────┐  ┌───────┐         │
              │   │ #104  │  │ #105  │  │ #106  │         │
              │   │ Tests │  │ Perf  │  │ Docs  │  ...    │
              │   │ [plan]│  │ [plan]│  │ [plan]│         │
              │   └───────┘  └───────┘  └───────┘         │
              │                                           │
              └───────────────────────────────────────────┘
```

Each block passes through the same phases. Each block gets the same
quality checks. The output is predictable because the process is
consistent — not because every issue is simple, but because every
issue is treated with the same rigor.

Sequant doesn't make Claude Code less flexible. It makes the workflow
around it more structured. You still get the full power of Claude Code
inside each phase. Sequant just ensures that power is applied
methodically — plan first, build in isolation, review before merging.

---

## Quick Start

```bash
npx sequant init
npx sequant doctor
```

Then in Claude Code:

```text
/fullsolve 123
```

See the [Quickstart Guide](../guides/quickstart.md) for the full walkthrough.

at your own pace.

```text

/qa 123            Review it
```

```
